content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string SSS. You can perform the following operations on SSS:


Flip: Pick an index iii (1≤i≤∣S∣)(1 \le i \le |S|)(1≤i≤∣S∣) and flip the iii-th character (i.e change 111 to 000 or 000 to 111). For e.g. 011‾001→010‾00101\underline{1}001 \rightarrow 01\underline{0}001011​001→010​001
Compress: Pick any non-empty substring consisting of the same character and replace it with a single occurrence of that character. For e.g. 1001111‾10→1001‾10100\underline{1111}10 \rightarrow 100\underline{1}101001111​10→1001​10
You want to make all the characters of the binary string equal (i.e. either all characters are 000 or all characters are 111). Find the minimum number of operations required to do so.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains a binary string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one integer - the minimum number of operations required to make all the characters of SSS equal.
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a binary string S consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of operations (flips and/or compresses) required to make all characters of S equal.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each string we compute runs. The cheapest way is to turn the string into all '1's for the first two cases (cost 2) and all '0's for the third case (cost 3) using the min(len,2) rule.
A single character is already uniform, so no operation is needed.
Runs are alternating single characters. To make all '0's we need to flip the three '1' runs (each length 1) → 3 flips. Making all '1's also costs 3. Minimum is 3.

solution.approach:
The string can be viewed as a sequence of runs (maximal substrings of equal characters). A compress operation can shrink any run of length L>1 to a single character in one operation, after which a flip can change that character. To turn a run of the opposite character into the target character we have two choices:
1. Flip every character of the run – cost L.
2. Compress the run to length 1 (cost 1) and then flip that single character (cost 1) – total cost 2.
Hence for a run of length L the cheapest way to make it uniform with the target character costs min(L, 2).
We compute the total cost for making the whole string all '0's (sum min(len,2) over all runs of '1') and similarly for all '1's. The answer is the smaller of the two totals.
The algorithm runs in O(|S|) per test case by a single scan that builds the runs and accumulates the two costs.
Time Complexity: O(total length of all strings). Space Complexity: O(1) besides the input storage.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Σ|S|) – linear in the total number of characters across all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    t = int(data[0])
    idx = 1
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        s = data[idx]; idx += 1
        cost0 = cost1 = 0
        i = 0
        n = len(s)
        while i < n:
            cur = s[i]
            j = i
            while j < n and s[j] == cur:
                j += 1
            length = j - i
            if cur == '0':
                cost1 += length if length < 2 else 2
            else:
                cost0 += length if length < 2 else 2
            i = j
        out_lines.append(str(min(cost0, cost1)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
